coredata.dc:title: Microstructure and tensile properties of as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy
coredata.prism:copyright: Copyright © 2005 Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Xu, X.J.
Lin, J.P.
Wang, Y.L.
Gao, J.F.
Lin, Z.
Chen, G.L.
coredata.dc:description: Microstructure and tensile properties of high Nb containing TiAl alloy, with nominal composition of Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) (at.%), were investigated. The results showed that the as-cast alloy had near lamellar microstructure composed of fine homogenous lamellar colonies, with a size of about 70μm, and a small amount of β phase and equiaxed γ phase. The β phase existed either on the boundaries of the lamellar colonies in irregular morphology or in the lamellar colonies in lamellar or blocky morphology. Equiaxed γ phase existed on the boundaries of lamellar colonies. There also existed some borides in three morphologies: convoluted flakes, ribbons and particles. The alloy had mediate tensile properties at both room temperature (RT) and high temperature. At RT, ultimate tensile strength level of 668MPa and 0.49% plastic elongation were obtained, and the brittle-to-ductile transition temperature was higher than 815°C. Cracks propagated in transgranular mode predominantly, and borides and β phase were detrimental to the ductility of the as-cast alloy.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Intermetallics
Casting
Microstructure
Tensile properties

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fractographies and microstructure after tensile tests
Effect of elements on the solidification
Effect of element and microstructure on the tensile properties

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The appearance of the ingot of Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy (Ø 120mm×420mm).
SEM-BSE images of the ingot of Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy: (a) the rim zone and (b) the center.
Transmission electron micrographs of lamellar microstructure.
Morphologies of β phase in as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy: (a) blocky morphology and (b) lamellar morphology.
Morphologies of borides in as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy: (a) convoluted flake, (b) ribbons and fine particles and (c) TEM many-beam image of particle.
Tensile properties as a function of the temperature for as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy.
SEM fractographies of as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy at 25°C (a), 600°C (b), 760°C (c) and 815°C (d).

Side view SEM-BSE fractographies of fracture specimens tested at 815°C for as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy: (a) microcrack at interface of boride and matrix and (b) microcrack at interface of β and matrix.
Compositions of boride and Y2O3 in as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy

article.ce:title: Microstructure and tensile properties of as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) alloy

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory for Advanced Metals and Materials, University of Science and Technology Beijing, Beijing 100083, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Microstructure and tensile properties of high Nb containing TiAl alloy, with nominal composition of Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) (at.%), were investigated. The results showed that the as-cast alloy had near lamellar microstructure composed of fine homogenous lamellar colonies, with a size of about 70μm, and a small amount of β phase and equiaxed γ phase. The β phase existed either on the boundaries of the lamellar colonies in irregular morphology or in the lamellar colonies in lamellar or blocky morphology. Equiaxed γ phase existed on the boundaries of lamellar colonies. There also existed some borides in three morphologies: convoluted flakes, ribbons and particles. The alloy had mediate tensile properties at both room temperature (RT) and high temperature. At RT, ultimate tensile strength level of 668MPa and 0.49% plastic elongation were obtained, and the brittle-to-ductile transition temperature was higher than 815°C. Cracks propagated in transgranular mode predominantly, and borides and β phase were detrimental to the ductility of the as-cast alloy.

ce:section.ce:para: Titanium aluminides based on the general composition (in at.%) Ti–45Al–(5–10)Nb exhibit several desired properties for high temperature applications involving the use as materials in aerospace and automotive engines. These alloys combine high mechanical strength and good corrosion resistance at elevated temperature with appreciable ductility at ambient temperature [1–4]. Thus, these materials are thought to extend the service range of conventional titanium aluminides towards high tresses and temperatures. However, the beneficial effect of Nb is compromised by problems in achieving chemical homogeneity due to the significantly higher melting temperatures. If these alloys are produced by conventional ingot metallurgy at moderately fast cooling, a significant primary segregation of the alloying elements occurs and the chemical inhomogeneity induces microstructure change [5]. The changed microstructure may be harmful to the tensile properties. Although thermomechanical processing and heat treatment can improve both the microstructure and properties, casting represents the most cost effective route to produce components. Therefore, study on as-cast microstructure and mechanical properties are essential for the casting components application of high Nb containing TiAl alloy. The present work aims at the investigation of microstructure and tensile properties of a newly developed high Nb containing TiAl ingot with large size.

The alloy with nominal composition Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) (at.%) was prepared using consumable electrode arc melting technique in an argon atmosphere, and remelted in a vacuum-skull melting furnace in order to reduce composition heterogeneity. An ingot with a diameter of 120mm and a height of 420mm was cast and then hot isostatic pressed (HIP) to eliminate casting porosity (Fig. 1 ). Chemical analyses indicated the composition of the ingot matched the nominal composition within the experimental error. The oxygen content was less than 550ppm.

Samples for microstructural investigations were taken from the center as well as from the rim of ingot. Microstructure analyses were performed utilizing scanning electron (SEM) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM). SEM studies were carried out using backscattered electron mode (BSE) to obtain contrast of different phase for phase volume fraction and grain size determination. TEM studies were carried out for lamellar spacing determination. The lamellar spacing given in the present paper was arithmetic average value measured without taking account of types of lamellar boundaries. Phases were determined by X-ray diffraction technique (XRD) and confirmed by energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS). For SEM observation, samples were ground and mechanically polished. TEM foil were prepared by twin jet electropolished in a solution of 60% (vol.%) methanol, 35% butyl alcohol and 5% perchloric acid at 15V and −30°C.

Tensile tests were conducted at 25–815°C in air on a MTS testing machine at a strain rate of 1×10−3 s−1. The specimens were prepared by electric discharge machining (EDM) in a form of plate with gauge size of 25mm×3mm×1.5mm and then electropolished. The specimens were taken from the center of the ingot with the tensile axis paralleling to the radial direction. The yield strength (YS), ultimate tensile strength (UTS) and the plastic elongation to failure (δ) were determined by a strip chart recording. After tests, the fracture surfaces of the specimens were observed via secondary electron imaging in a SEM, and microstructure below the fracture surfaces was observed via BSE in a SEM.

Fig. 6 shows the temperature dependence of the tensile properties of the alloy. It can be seen that the alloy has a mediate strength and ductility. With the increase of the temperature, YS, UTS increase and δ decreases. The tensile strength shows only a small decrease from ∼668MPa at RT to ∼595MPa at 815°C. The elongation increases from 0.49% to 1.42% when the temperature is increased from RT to 815°C, showing on brittle-to-ductile transition (BDT).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fractographies and microstructure after tensile tests
ce:section.ce:para: Fig. 7 shows the SEM images of fracture surfaces of tensile specimens tested at different temperatures. The fracture mode is brittle exhibiting translamellar fracture predominantly, and delamenation or interlamellar fracture. Long boride ribbons are frequently observed for fracture surface both at RT and high temperatures (shown by arrows). It is worth noting that the long ribbons lie on the fracture surfaces approximately perpendicular to the tensile axis. Most of the boride ribbons have been pulled out of the metal matrix during failure, leaving indentations on the fracture surface. The inside surfaces of the indentations are very smooth, indicating a debonding process along the interface between boride ribbons and the matrix. It seems reasonable to deduce that the low ductility is related to these long boride ribbons.

Fig. 8 shows the side views of microstructures below the fracture surfaces of the specimens tested at 815°C. The microstructure is not different from the as-cast state. Colony boundary sliding is not obviously activated. The main crack and secondary cracks propagate mainly through the lamellar colonies, and the microcracks formed along interfaces of the boride/matrix and β/matrix are clearly seen.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of elements on the solidification
ce:section.ce:para:
The ingot has uniform fine-grained NL microstructure composed of lamellar colonies with wider lamellar spacing and γ and β grains. This is mainly attributed to the two reasons: (1) solidification path induced by high Nb addition and (2) grain refinement effect of minor boron addition. According to our previous result [4], 8–9at.% Nb addition induce the solidification and solid-state transformation path way for the as-cast microstructure of TiAl alloy to be: L→L+β→β→β+α→α→α+γ→lamella (α2 +γ)+γ. The alloys that solidify through the β phase show a columnar character much less pronounced [7]. The reason is that although the 〈100〉 axis of the β phase is the preferential direction of crystal growth during solidification, there are three equivalent directions for 〈100〉, namely [100], [010] and [001]. This is different from the case of α-solidification where there is only one orientation (the C axis) for preferential crystal growth. During cooling after solidification, β crystals are transformed to α, at least partly. This transformation most likely takes place by following the orientation relationship of
{ 1
1 ¯
0 }
β
/ /
( 0
0
0
1 )
α
and
1
1
1
β
/ /
1
1
2 ¯
0
α
. As a result, in theory, twelve variants of α with different orientations form. During subsequent cooling, the γ phase precipitates in a lamellar form in each α variant. The resultant microstructure is characterized by the presence of some colonies corresponding to the α variants inside each initial β grain. Moreover, each of these colonies comprises numerous α colonies and these colonies are often surrounded by some residual β phase. Finally, inside each colony, one can observe the γ+α2 lamellar structure. This may be one reason of the fine cast microstructure observed in the present alloy. On the other hand, the alloy contains minor boron addition. Because of the low solubility of boron in the β phase, more boron solute will be rejected into the liquid as the alloy solidifies. The constitutional supercooling ahead of the solid front would, therefore, be very significant. It is known that under conditions of extreme constitutional supercooling, nucleation could take place in the liquid ahead of the solidification front. Such nucleation would be responsible for boron-induced grain refinement in TiAl alloy. The lamellar formation in the alloy will start at relatively low undercooling, i.e. at a relative high temperature. The width of the lamellae greatly depends on the temperature at which they form [8]. Due to the increased diffusivity and consequently enhanced the mobility of the interfacial dislocations and ledge, a courser lamellar structure is obtained in the boride containing TiAl alloy.

Our previous work also shows that in the alloy with Nb addition above 9.5at.%, there exists a three phase (α2 +γ+β) field in the high Nb containing TiAl alloy[4]. Thus, it is suspected that the β phase precipitates again from the α phase in the regions rich in Nb. Therefore, Apart from the residual β phase presenting at colony boundaries and triple junctions, β phase also exists in the lamellar colonies.

It is found by other investigator, that borides exist in different morphologies [9,10]. The crystal structure and the composition of the boride depend on the boron content and other factors. For the studied alloy, the crystal structure of the flakes and ribbons is not known yet, but from the appearance of the particles it is concluded that the boride phase is TiB2. It is thought that the particles nucleate and grow as primary β phase forms, and the flakes and ribbons grow concurrently with the melt.

The existence of Y2O3 particles suggests that the solubility of Y in TiAl alloys with high Nb content is very small. The oxygen atoms of oxides come from the original oxygen of master alloy or the melting process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of element and microstructure on the tensile properties
ce:section.ce:para: For as-forged Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) (at.%) alloy, the strength is higher than that of conventional TiAl alloy and lower than that of as-forged material. The higher strength compared with conventional TiAl is attributed to Nb addition and finer grain size. The lower strength compared with as-forged material is attributed to coarser grain size and wider lamellar spacing. Generally, high Nb containing TiAl alloys exhibit poor ductility at RT, especially with lamellar structure. For the studied alloy, 0.49% elongation at RT is higher compared with the alloys developed by others [10]. This is attributed to the fine grain size, which is beneficial to both strength and ductility. Borides often contain defects. These defects are likely to cause stress concentration within the ribbon especially when they lie in tensile specimens with their broad surface perpendicular to the tensile axis. The cracks in the poorly orientated boride ribbons can spread through their whole lengths instantaneously due to their brittle nature. The deformation incompatibility of β phase and matrix will induce microcracks between their interfaces and reduce the ductility of the alloy as well. It is implied that boron addition has contradictory effects on tensile ductility. Finer grain induced by boron addition is beneficial to ductility, but lager boride precipitation is detrimental to the ductility.

ce:list-item.ce:para: The as-cast Ti–45Al–(8–9)Nb–(W, B, Y) (at.%) alloy had near lamellar microstructure composed of fine homogenous lamellar colonies with a lamellar spacing of bout 700nm and a small amount of β phase and equiaxed γ phase. The β phase existed either on the boundaries of the lamellar colonies or in the lamellar colonies in platelet or blocky morphology. Equiaxed γ phase existed on the boundaries of lamellar colonies. The borides appeared in three morphologies: convoluted flakes, ribbons and particles. There also has some Y2O3 precipitates.

The alloy had mediate strength and limited ductility at both RT and high temperatures and the brittle-to-ductile transition temperature was higher than 815°C. Cracks propagated in translamellar mode predominantly and microcracks formed in the interfaces of borides/matrix and β phase/matrix. The β phase and borides were detrimental to the ductility of the as-cast alloy.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was sponsored by the State Key Project for Fundamental Research Developing Plan, under contract No. G2000067206-2 and National Natural Science Foundation of China, under contract No. 50274005 and No. 50471097.
